videos.title: 20 Things to Do in EDINBURGH, Scotland | 1st timers guide!
videos.description:
Join me on a 3-day solo adventure to Edinburgh, Scotland. I'll take you on a tour of the must-see sights, provide some tips on how to navigate the city and highlight a few hidden gems! Edinburgh was truly a dream city and, though I could have spent days more there, a weekend trip was the perfect amount of time to get a full taste!

View a MAP of my complete itinerary and recommendations here (85+ pinned spots!): https://www.paypal.com/donate/?hosted_button_id=6GVEP4RPNA77U

See how I planned this trip: https://youtu.be/siPq-8zRRik?si=0Giu7_7VyyEZppFz

CHAPTERS
0:00 - Introduction
0:31 - Edinburgh overview/map
1:35 - InterContinental Edinburgh The George
2:17 - The Scran & Scallie
3:26 - Rose Street
3:49 - Dean Village
4:44 - Stockbridge Market
5:27 - Royal Botanic Garden
7:36 - The Royal Yacht Brittania
8:51 - Calton Hill
9:46 - The Piper's Rest
10:07 - Mary King's Close
10:38 - The Royal Oak
11:04 - The Royal Mile
13:59 - The Palace of Holyroodhouse
15:06 - Arthur's Seat
15:50 - The Fudge House
16:09 - Edinburgh Castle
17:24 - The Witchery

videos.transcript: Hey! My name is Ashley and I just wrapped up a 72-hour solo trip to Edinburgh, Scotland. This was my first time visiting this historic city... ...and I'm happy to share with you some travel tips I learned along the way... ...suggest things for you to see and do on your visit... ...and take you on a quick tour of my accommodations. Let's jump into it. Edinburgh is situated along the eastern coast of Scotland and is the capital of the country. If you're flying in, Edinburgh Airport is just inside the city limits... ...with a simple tram service getting you to the city center in just about 40 minutes. I purchased my round trip ticket in advance and was on the tram in no time. You can also purchase tickets from the local kiosks or the app. The tram system is super intuitive, with only one line... ...running back and forth through the city. At several points, you can hop off in favor of commuter rails and a... ...robust bus network, or you can forgo all of this in favor of a rental car... ...but, do keep in mind that folks here drive on the left side of the road. Honestly speaking, I found the city to be highly walkable... ...so, if you don't mind a few hills, I'd say a rental car is really not necessary. For most visitors, you'll likely be staying in the New Town or Old Town areas... ...as those appear to hold the greatest concentration of notable sights and... ...make up Edinburgh’s UNESCO World Heritage Site. I booked the Intercontinental Edinburgh The George... ...which is an IHG hotel in New Town. The foundation of this hotel dates back to 1766... ...but, the bank-like facade that we see now was built in 1840... ...as part of the Caledonian Insurance Company. I loved walking through the hallways, hearing the creaking of the wood floors... ...below the carpet. It brought out a certain charm that just completely set the scene for my time in Edinburgh. And, here's a look at my room specifically, which I found to be cozy and clean. After settling in, I paid a visit to Andrew at the concierge desk, who sat with me... ...for about 30 minutes detailing the most popular things I needed to see and do... ...and giving me his personal recommendations for places he frequents as a local. What I share with you now is thanks in large part to Andrew’s suggestions... ...and I'm just going to go in the order I did things, not by any particular ranking system. With jetlag creeping in, my first stop was a sunset stroll across town for dinner. Nestled within the Stockbridge neighborhood, The Scran & Scallie is... ...a gastropub featuring delectable Scottish food sourced from local suppliers. I indulged in a haggis dish alongside seared Orkney scallops... ...and was convinced into a whiskey tasting, making this entire meal... ...in my humble opinion, the perfect introduction to Scotland. My favorite whiskey of the three was this one. If you're seeking a bit of nightlife, you might want to check out Rose Street in New Town... ...which I stumbled upon on my walk back to the hotel. This felt like a local college hangout, but did have a good mix of ages... ...and was a bustling thoroughfare of pubs and music venues. The Black Cat was recommended to me for its authentic Scottish music vibes... ...but, it was pretty quiet when I popped in. The next day was a day. I walked about 15 miles round trip, hitting some of Edinburgh's lesser visited sites while truly... ...trying to take in the local communities. Up fairly early, I began my morning in Dean Village... ...a quintessential milling community founded in the 12th century. What drew me to this location was its picturesque architecture... ...and distinctly quaint neighborhood feels. It was, aesthetically a town preserved in time, yet... ...modern life was evident throughout. From here, I descended upon the Water of Leith Walkway -- a well-maintained 13-mile path... ...offering a peaceful promenade past many areas of interest. You can join the path at a number of different spots... ...and even enjoy an audio trail guide along the way. I walked the path for a short distance before exiting at the Stockbridge Market... ...open every Sunday from 10 to 4. I must say I had high hopes for this market and it did not disappoint. It was fairly small, but it was packed with enthusiastic locals and visitors alike.... ...conversing with various vendors, selling meats, cheeses, whiskey, homemade products and a whole lot more. I bought an apple tart from this stand. So good. And then cheese from this booth. We all need a block of cheese to ourselves from time to time, no? Goodies in tow, I continued my walk through town and specifically through the iconic Circus Lane... ...before eventually joining back up with the Water of Leith Walkway. A short distance later, I arrived at the Royal Botanic Garden... ...featuring 70 acres of beautiful landscape dating back over 350 years. It actually had its start as a physic garden, growing medicinal plants near Holyrood Abbey... ...but has since evolved as a global center for biodiversity science and horticulture. This was such a peaceful retreat. I spent about two hours casually perusing the various areas of the garden... ...and locating benches hidden within the foliage to reflect. They even had a little shop at the entrance... ...where I secured a pouch of Scottish thistle seeds to bring back home to grow. Exiting the garden, I connected back up with the Water of Leith Walkway... ...this time noticing how distinctly local it felt along this section of path. There were no tourists over here... ...just Scottish folks enjoying the warming days of spring. One particular area that caught my eye was this tiered hillside... ...which I later learned was the Warriston Allotments. Originally created for unemployed families during the 1920s depression... ...the city uses the space today, renting out plots to local growers on a yearly basis. According to their website, there's apparently an 8-year waitlist to secure a plot. Without too much fanfare, I finally arrived in Leith... ...an industrial port district at the water's edge. Since arriving home, I've realized that I may have missed the nicer area of Leith... ...because my experience led me through a rather rundown and sad collection of city streets. This was apparently the place to be. I, on the other hand, was on my way to the Royal Yacht Britannia... ...and that's how I ended up here. If you enjoy following all things royal... ...or perhaps indulged in a few too many binges of The Crown on Netflix... ...you'll know how important the Royal Yacht Britannia was to the late Queen Elizabeth II. Launching in 1953, the Yacht spent 44 years in royal service... ...calling at over 600 ports and 135 countries. Today, the ship remains tethered to the Scottish harbor, allowing visitors... ...to browse its 5 decks and peer into carefully preserved cabins. 95% of items on display are original artifacts on loan from the Royal Collection Trust. This tour is led by a hand-held audio guide, which I found to be super informative. It kept the flow of people moving at a nice pace... ...allowing me to really soak in the different spaces. If you've got kids, have no fear... ...there is a lot of interesting things for them to look at, but I think they'll... ...find the adorable Corgi treasure hunt to be a particularly fun game to play. And, if you're hoping for a more lavish experience, well, the ship... ...today has a hotel on board, an event space, and even a tea room. I’d count this is a must-see attraction for your time in Edinburgh. After a quick break back at my hotel, I was keen to find the best view of the city... ...and made my way over to Calton Hill. This was one of 3 spots recommended to me touting... ...its panorama views of Edinburgh, and though I haven't shown you the other two yet... ...this was, hands down, my favorite. Calton Hill is a short 5-minute climb up from the New Town area... ...and is host to the City Observatory and National Monument. It offers a completely unobstructed view of Edinburgh and surrounding neighborhoods. What I found most intriguing was the geological features of the city. Prior to this trip, I had no idea Edinburgh was nestled within such a volcanic landscape... ...and now I'd consider it to be one of its most defining characteristics. Next up was dinner at The Piper's Rest in Old Town, located just next to the Royal Mile. Don't worry, I plan to fully explore this tomorrow morning without the crowds... ...so, more to come on that... ...but, in the meantime, The Piper's Rest was a great casual dining venue... ...with live music, good vibes and a tasty burger. To close out the night, I made my way over to Mary King's Close... ...a ticketed tour through Edinburgh's only preserved 17th century street... ...a place which endured overcrowding, plagues and so much more. Photos and video were, unfortunately, not allowed inside, but I'll share with you... ...some tidbits I learned as we walk the Royal Mile in the morning. I’d highly recommend this tour if you're into history... ...and would even encourage you to book it at the start of your trip... ...to get a full appreciation for the city as a whole. On my way back to the hotel, I stopped by The Royal Oak... ...a 200 year old pub known for its lively folk music and spontaneous performances. I was told this was another great place to hear some authentic Scottish music... ...but there was a ticketed event going on this evening... ...so I struck out again on night time entertainment. It was probably for the better, because the next morning was set to be an early one. Up for sunrise and out as the local milkmen made their rounds... ...I began my walk from New Town up to Old Town to finally explore the Royal Mile. The Royal Mile is often considered to be the backbone of the city... ...stretching 1.6km between Edinburgh Castle and the Palace of Holyroodhouse. Chances are you've already seen it... ...if you happened to watch Queen Elizabeth II’s funeral procession... ...which made its way up from the palace to St. Giles Cathedral. The Royal Mile is packed with history and is also the central spot for tourism in the city. I’d dare to consider it the Times Square of Edinburgh... ...which is why I chose to explore it so early without the crowds... ...apparently even earlier than garbage pickup. For those of you wondering, I think the best direction to walk this street is from the castle... ...to the palace, because you will be heading downhill. So here I am, starting in the castle parking lot. We'll be back later to explore inside, but for now... ...I enjoyed the views of the sleepy city below. Edinburgh, at one time, was a walled city defending its occupants from attack and invasion. The upper part of the Royal Mile sat squarely within this walled section... ...and evidence of its Medieval past is easily recognizable... ...from its cobblestone streets to its public execution sites, and narrow closes. A close, like Mary King's Close that I visited last night, is a small alleyway... ...that once led residents to their homes deeper off the Royal Mile. The closes were often named after a notable person who resided there, but not always. As a visitor, you can wander down many closes, but just keep in mind that... ...these are still mostly residential areas. For a more public facing place to explore... ...be sure to pay a visit to Victoria Street, a colorful and charming thoroughfare... ...filled with quirky shops along its winding corridor. This was just steps away from the Royal Mile. As I continued down toward the Palace of Holyroodhouse... ...I was met with other early risers awaiting their tours for the day... ...and, just beyond them, I stumbled upon the World’s End pub. Definitely too early for a pint, but I stopped here to observe the brass plates in the road... ...signifying the former gates built into the walls of the city. It was particularly interesting, because there was a definitive change of... ...character along the Royal Mile on the other side of this. There were far less shops, a paved road, and a more contemporary residential feel. Eventually I arrived at Holyrood Palace... ...the official residence of the royal family in Edinburgh. It is home to over 900 years of Scottish royal history... ...including playing host to many significant events during the short reign of Mary, Queen of Scots. When the royals are out of town, the palace is open to the public... ...for a guided audio tour through some of the most historic chambers of the complex. Completely unintentional, I ended up being the very first visitor of the day... ...and if you want to know what it feels like to have your own palace... ...I'd recommend you try to be there when it opens as well. Unfortunately, no photos were allowed inside... ...but, the experience of wandering room to room alone will be one I'll never forget. Outside, you can pay a visit to Holyrood Abbey, dating back to 1128... ...and take in the vibrant gardens. Looming over the palace is Arthur's Seat... ...an extinct volcano offering sweeping views of the city below. With several trails to choose from... ...I embarked on a steep hike from the palace up the hillside... ...and found the path to be quite rocky and crowded. I don't know about you, but I love hiking for its peace and solitude... ...and I'll just say that this experience definitely did not bring those. This was an unexpectedly popular spot. In fact, I stopped myself from fully reaching the summit... ...because there were just too many people. With that being said, the views from this point were still enjoyable... ...but, for me, Calton Hill remains the definitive winner. Ready for a snack and a nap... ...I wanted to give a shout out to The Fudge House back on the Royal Mile... ...which I passed as I was returning to my hotel. This shop is run by third generation makers of traditional Scottish fudge. The staff was incredibly friendly and it was some of the best fudge I've ever tried. After a few hours catching up on sleep, I was out again to attend my time slot -- ...the last one of the day -- at Edinburgh Castle. The castle is one of the oldest fortified places in Europe... ...with a long history as a military base, a royal residence, prison and fortress. Besides the strength of the structure... ...its placement atop another dormant volcano solidifies itself as a true beacon... of the cityscape and one of Edinburgh's most visited sights. Once inside, there is so much to explore, including various museums... ...the Royal Palace, the Crown Jewels and St. Margaret's Chapel... ...the city's oldest building. Unfortunately, the chapel was so crowded during my attempt to peer inside... ...that I was forced to turn around. Sadly, much of my experience at the castle was met with a high crowd volume... ...making this a pretty taxing attraction. I chose to spend my time focusing on the outside spaces.. ...taking in the architecture and city views. A highlight for me as I explored these buildings was learning... ...that the national animal of Scotland is the unicorn. Could I love this place even more? To wrap up my last evening in Edinburgh, I booked a dining reservation at The Witchery... ...located right near the castle. From the outside, the restaurant exudes Medieval vibes... ...and is actually nestled within a handful of 16th century buildings. Its name is derived from the hundreds of men and women... ...burned at the stake as witches on Castlehill during the 1500s and 1600s. Inside the Secret Garden, where I was seated, was a lavish and dimly lit dining room... ...easily making this my most romantic solo dinner ever. And boy, did I spoil myself. I enjoyed an aged Scotch beef tartare appetizer with a Pinot Grigio... ...a pulled jackfruit and butternut squash lasagna... ...a chocolate torte and a green tea. This meal was the perfect way to wrap up an incredible 72 hours in Edinburgh. If you’d like to follow my itinerary or at least keep a running list... ...of suggested spots in your back pocket for your trip, feel free to visit... ...the link in my description for a pinned map of my adventure. As always, thanks so much for watching.

videos.title: Mistakes Tourists Make in Scotland
videos.description:
Heading to the Isle of Skye or Edinburgh or Aberdeen and not sure what to expect in Scotland? Well we have put together a list of the best or worst travel mistakes that tourists make when they visit Scotland. You may see Nessie at Loch Ness, but don't spend all your vacation there! From missing out on Haggis to trying to understand the Highland accent there are a few fun and not so fun mistakes tourists make in Scotland. Honest travel advice on visiting Scotland. 
Filmed in Scotland on the Shetland Islands

videos.transcript: Hey there, fellow travelers. Mark here with Wolters World. Today, we're here in Lerwick on the Shetland Islands in northern Scotland. And today, what we have for you are some of the common mistakes that tourists make when they come here to Scotland because this is a fantastic country. You're really going to enjoy it, but there's a few kind of mistakes, faux pas, things you might want to know before you come here so you don't make those mistakes so you can have a better time when you're here. And the first mistake I think tourists make when they come here is they don't buy the passes. Look, when you're going to go see the sights, a lot of times there's passes that will include multiple sites that you can go see that'll be on one ticket. Maybe you'll get a two-day pass or you can buy a membership. There's Historic Scotland, there's the National Trust for Scotland, and they'll actually sell passes for you that'll get you in the entrance into all kinds of really great sites. And the thing is, if you're going to see more than like two of the sites, it pays for itself if you just get like a short like, you know, travelers ticket kind of stuff. It's well worth doing it, so make sure you're saving some money that way. Don't leave money on the table, okay? Now, the second mistake we see tourists make when they come here is they think that everyone will be glad to take their Scottish pounds when they leave Scotland. For those who don't know, you use the British pound here in Scotland as a currency, but there's also special notes from Scottish banks, from the Royal Bank of Scotland, the Bank of Scotland, Clydesdale Bank that are Scottish pounds. So, you can have four different versions of a 20-pound note or a 10-pound note. And the thing is, when you leave Scotland, not everybody really likes the Scottish pounds. Now, everywhere in Scotland people will take it, and most places in England they'll take them as well. Some people might not, like they might not be too happy with it, but it's just one of those things to think about. But the big issue is when you leave the UK and you try to exchange them at an exchange house, like if you're in Hungary going there and trying to exchange, they might not take them. So, it's best when you're leaving to try to get some of the the pounds to say from Bank of England instead of the Bank of Scotland, Royal Bank of Scotland, or or Clydesdale Bank. So, just have a heads-up on that one. Now, my next mistake actually is going to deal with Edinburgh cuz I know most of the tourists that come to Scotland just go to Edinburgh and kind of take off. And so, I think a good mistake I see people make is when they see the tourist map of Edinburgh, they think it's flat there. Look, you need to understand that tourist map of Edinburgh makes it look so simple to get around, but you have some pretty big hills you're going to have to deal with when you're there. So, make sure before you come to Scotland, practice up on some hiking and some walking cuz you're going to have a little hill to go up that Royal Mile. It starts here and ends up here, okay? So, do have a heads-up for that one. Now, the next mistake we see tourists make when they come here is buying a kilt. Look, if you want to buy a kilt, that's great, fantastic, go ahead, help the Scottish economy. I know they appreciate it, so do that. But, the thing is is one, when are you going to wear a kilt outside of Scotland, okay? That's one of the things cuz they're not cheap to get. Like, you're going to spend some money to get a nice kilt. And the thing is is don't just buy one randomly. If you have Scottish history or Scottish ancestry like we do, we have multiple families that are Scottish in our ancestry, so we can choose different kind of, you know, patterns or tartans that we could wear for for our kilts. The thing is, if you have a history and you want to buy one, find out which clan you are and get that one cuz you don't want to have the wrong one out there. Also, a lot of times you'll see tourists that will buy them, they have no clue how to wear them. So, make sure if you do buy one, don't make the mistake of not having them teach you how to wear it so you actually can wear it the right way. And kind of going along with that is this mistake that people think that Scots really wear kilts all the time. Look, they they don't wear kilts all the time. It's more like if they work in the tourism industry, they might have it on or if there's like a formal gathering like a wedding or something like that, then they might wear them. But, in general, yeah, you're not seeing too many kilts around, all right? Another mistake that tourists make when they come here to Scotland is they just go to Edinburgh and Glasgow with maybe a day trip to Stirling in between, and then they take off. You really need to get out and explore Scotland. This place is gorgeous. We're here in the Shetland Islands and you know, seeing the cliffs up here, seeing all the islands and the coastline, seeing the stone structures, the blocks and stuff like that. It's so cool. Trying to go find Nessie at Loch Ness, going through the Isle of Skye and see the waterfalls there. Oh my gosh, the formations there. Going through the Highlands, it's just such a beautiful country and you got to get out there and enjoy it. And the thing is though, most people just stick Edinburgh, Glasgow, maybe Stirling and take off. You need to get out more and I think the reason why is actually probably our next mistake and that mistake is people give up on the thought of driving here in Scotland once they hear they drive on the left side. I cannot tell you how many people have written to us and called us and talked to us saying, "I'd love to go to Scotland, but I'm scared to drive on the left hand side side of the road." So they don't come. The thing is is don't worry. What I recommend, watch some videos online about driving on the left side. That will make you feel better. Take your time when you're here. Have patience. Concentrate. There's no filming yourself, "Look, I'm I'm driving on the other side of the road." No, you're not doing that, all right? Don't do those things. Focus on the road. Get a good GPS for the car. The phone ones aren't as good as the car ones, so get that so you feel more comfortable driving around and it will make it a lot easier cuz believe me, there's so many castles and and sites to go see throughout the country that you can't really get to very well with public transportation or can't get to at all that you really really need to have your own transportation. So don't make the mistake of not renting a car. Now, another common mistake I see tourists make is they don't even give Scottish food a chance. Look, Scotland actually has some really good food. The seafood here, the salmon, the mussels, the scallops, the other fish, I mean the white fish we've had here. I mean there's been so much good food here and you're going to eat very well in Scotland. So don't be like, "Oh, going to Scotland looks like I'm going to lose 10 lb unless I drink my dinner." No, look, you can eat really well. I mean, the seafood's great. The beef here is outstanding. The lamb, oh, oh, you got to do the lamb when you're here. And there's all kinds of other fun stuff. You can have the local whiskey that's here. You have the Iron Brew, that's the Scottish soda you that I'll talk about later. You have that. And there's all kinds of really good Scottish traditional cuisine you can have and enjoy when you're here. And I think enjoying that food is so important that there's another mistake that tourists make, and that is not making reservations for dinner on Friday and Saturday nights or not making reservations for dinner during the Edinburgh Festival in Edinburgh in August, or not making reservations for dinner anytime you're in a small town during tourist season. Cuz there's a limited number of restaurants and spots and stuff like that. And if you want to guarantee you have a place to eat or there's a restaurant you really want to go to have your Shetland mussels or your Orkney scallops or whatever, you got to make sure you're making those reservations. Even if you're in a small town by a popular tourist place. We were staying in Plockton a few years ago, and there like we booked our accommodation 4 months before we actually were going to stay there, and the lady's like, "Hey, I'm going to make your reservations at this restaurant so you know you have a place to eat when you're here cuz there's limited spots." So, don't forget to make those reservations for dinner when you are coming, okay? And it's not just in big cities, it's in small towns as well. Also, probably don't forget to make hotel reservations if you're going to be going to smaller towns and going to drive around the country so you can kind of plan out your route before you go so you're a bit more comfortable with that drive. Now, changing topics for a little bit, I think another important mistake not to make when you're here is don't make the mistake of calling a Scotsman an Englishman. They're they're they're they're not going to appreciate. A Scot is a Scot, English is English. Make sure you separate them because they will let you know that they are not English, they're Scottish. You might be able to get away with British, that's okay cuz it's part of Great Britain, but just know Scottish, Scotland, we're Scottish, okay? So, just know that one. And one of the ways you'll be able to tell they're Scottish instead of English is the accent. And that's another mistake I think tourists make is they go, "Hey, I'm going to Scotland. They speak English. I'm going to understand them just fine." Look, that's a mistake right there. The farther you go north in Scotland, the stronger the accent gets. And you'll see in some movies when the people are speaking like Scot- Scottish English, you know, in Scotland in the north, they might actually put subtitles down at the bottom to help people out. You might need a few times to have, "Excuse me, could you repeat that? Sorry, what did you say?" So, don't make the mistake of getting upset cuz you can't understand them. Just take some time and a smile. And know you're not the only tourist who didn't quite understand everything they said, okay? videos.title: 12 Things Not To Do In Edinburgh
videos.description:
What are the don'ts of visiting Edinburgh, Scotland? Visit Edinburgh, a city that is a treasure trove of history and culture and is well worth the trip. The fact that it is so unique, though, also means that there are many chances for you to make a fool of yourself while going about it. 
When traveling to Edinburgh, following are some of the most typical errors that tourists make. But don’t worry! The worst thing that may happen to you as a result of making any of these blunders is catching a cold and perhaps getting some funny stares from the locals. However, being well-prepared will not only make your trip to the Scottish capital more pleasurable but can also save you a little bit of money and clear up some of the misunderstanding you may encounter there.
1. Putting in an order for a single malt whiskey served over ice
Or even worse, with a Coca-Cola
It is easy to see why Scots are so proud of their whiskey. A complicated beverage that is as refined as any French wine has been crafted by using generations' worth of experience and years' worth of maturing. If you ask for your single malt to be served on the rocks, a knowledgeable bartender will highly recommend that you drink it neat first (the way it was intended to be served), and if required, add a few drops of water to your glass. Those with less discernment will put the ice in the glass, but they will be evaluating you while they do so. If you spoil the flavor of a perfectly nice dram by adding Coke to it, the grading will be that much more rigorous.
2. You shouldn't just drink whiskey all the time.
During your time in Edinburgh, you should definitely have a few drams of the "water of life," but save the distillery tours for when you're in the Highlands and Islands. It's possible that this picture contains Table Chairs for Restaurants Cafes and Dining Tables Furniture Chairs Wooden Chamber and the Interior.
Gin is big in Edinburgh: It is reported that the city has the highest per capita consumption of gin in the whole United Kingdom. You may take a tour of the Edinburgh Gin distillery, and then go to the Heads & Tales pub for a drink afterward. Similarly, in the Summerhall arts theatre's Dick's Bar, you can have Pickering's Gin, which is produced in the building next door, straight from the tap. The 56 North gin stockpile is the most extensive in all of Scotland, including more than 170 individual bottles.
3. Ignoring the raincoat
Even during the warmest months of the year, the weather in Scotland is famously unpredictable. It's been stated that whether the sky is clear or overcast, you shouldn't put too much stock in either one since you never know what the weather may bring. Even if you want to travel to Edinburgh in the middle of the summer, you should still bring a decent raincoat that is waterproof with you. Otherwise, you run the risk of being soaked. It is not worth your time to make an effort to control the situation using an umbrella since it will not hold up for long in the often-strong gusts.

Travelling to Edinburgh for the first time? In need of Edinburgh travel advice that caters to a newbie? This Edinburgh travel guide is packed with must-knows before you visit Edinburgh as a tourist, with a rundown of common tourist mistakes in Edinburgh as well as answers to common questions like Edinburgh culture shocks, Edinburgh culture and etiquette tips, where to go in Edinburgh, places to visit in Edinburgh, and more so you can have the Edinburgh trip of your dreams. Whether you're looking for general Edinburgh tips or tips for Edinburgh hidden gems, be sure to watch this video!
videos.transcript: if you are visiting Edinburgh for the first time, make sure you watch this video. Hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com and I recently spent an amazing month in Edinburgh, Scotland, exploring all the best that this city has to offer. Of course, I did learn a lot the hard way so in this video I'm going to be walking you through all my best tips and must-knows for first-timers. Now let's start with some basic must-knows and travel planning tips. First things first, we have to tackle the pronunciation of the city's name because a lot of people get it wrong. So to make sure we're all on the same page, let it be known that this city is pronounced like Edinbruh, not Edinboro, Edenburg or any variation like that, Edinburgh. Of course, there are some differences when it comes to accents and things like that but Edinburgh is a pretty safe approximation. Now let's move on to our next important tip, which is to book ahead. Edinburgh is a very beautiful but compact and busy city, so oftentimes hotel prices might be a lot higher than you might be used to looking at other destinations, so your best defense against these sky-high prices is to simply book as early as possible, especially when it comes to peak season. On that note, it's also very important that you know when peak season is, so generally speaking, the busiest periods in Edinburgh tend to be focused around the different festival seasons of the year, namely August and also around Christmas and leading up to New Year. August is an especially busy time because that's when Edinburgh hosts a bunch of different festivals including the Edinburgh Festival Fringe, which is actually the largest performing arts festival in the entire world, so needless to say, accommodation can be in quite short supply so your best defense, really, if you do want to be in town for festival season is to book as early as possible. If on the other hand, you're not really interested in attending these events, then I highly recommend booking your trip outside of these periods namely during shoulder season, so either spring or fall. That way you can avoid the chaos of the crowds because it can honestly be very, very overwhelming. Now another important consideration, when you're booking accommodation, is the area. Luckily Edinburgh is quite small and compact compared to other European capitals, so generally speaking, if you're staying within the city then you're never going to be too far away from any of the main attractions, especially if you're close to a tram stop or bus stop. That said, if you find a really good deal that seems a little bit further out, so long as you're close to public transport you're never too far away from the main sites, so I would say definitely go for it. Of course, if your budget allows for it, staying in the central core of Edinburgh will keep you within walking distance of most major sites, just beware of areas like Old Town which can be really, really hilly because at the end of the day, you know, after a long day of sightseeing the last thing you want to do is huff and puff your way back to the hotel, so just keep that in mind, that there's a lot of hills so you should take that into consideration. Now let's move on to Edinburgh transport tips and tips for getting around. In Edinburgh, you'll find two main train stations: Waverley and Haymarket. Waverley is the larger one in the city center, with 20 platforms and connections to pretty much anywhere you need to go in Scotland. It's also connected to a shopping center, so if it's your first time there I would highly recommend getting there well in advance just to make sure you have ample time to find where the train platforms are because a lot of visitors do struggle with that. Haymarket on the other hand, is a lot smaller and more manageable. So in any case, just keep in mind that there are two stations that are connected, so be sure to book for the one that's closer to where you're staying. Now in terms of getting around the city, your main options are buses, trams and taxis. With the local bus network, buses run 24 hours a day, with Lothian buses being the most popular company with 50-plus routes across the city. Another one you'll see a lot is First. These different companies operate on the same routes but with different bus numbers and also different tickets. Now this might seem confusing but it's honestly quite easy to simplify, just because these days the most common way to pay for the bus as a visitor, is just by using contactless card, so regardless of which bus it is you can just hop on board, tap in and then they'll charge you the correct fare. Another great thing about paying with contactless card is a lot of these bus companies actually have daily and weekly caps in place, to make sure that you're never paying more than a certain amount. So with Lothian buses, for instance, this program is known as TapTapCap, which is very cute. First also has a program called Tap&Cap, which operates in a similar way. You can also pay with cash on board but just take note that they don't give you any change, so you need to make sure you have the exact fare on you. Now trams are a little bit more complicated. Trams in Edinburgh only run from 5:30 in the morning until 11:30 at night and you have to make sure you buy a ticket in advance, either through the machines on the platforms or through their app or online. You also have to validate these tickets once you get on board, so it's a little bit more complicated than riding the bus but also you don't have traffic to contend with, so there's definitely pros there as well. Lastly, if you plan to combine bus travel and tram travel while you're in Edinburgh, you can also look into day tickets or Ridacards that allow you to combine modes of transport for a set fee, either for a day, a week or a month. These passes tend to include various modes of transport like Edinburgh trams, Lothian buses, the airport bus and a few others, so this can be an especially good deal if you're going to be taking public transport to and from the airport. All that said, once you're in the city center, I personally think that the best way to get around is just by foot. Honestly, Edinburgh is such a beautiful city and the more that you walk around, the more you really get to appreciate all the beautiful little streets and hidden secrets that there are. Sometimes it's even faster to walk versus taking the bus or a taxi, just because traffic can be really bad. Of course, it's worth saying if you do plan to do a lot of walking, Edinburgh is incredibly hilly, especially in the Old Town area as I mentioned before, so make sure you plan your walks strategically to avoid doubling back because it can be really exhausting to climb down some stairs because you thought it was pretty and then you realize you have to climb back up in a few minutes just to get to your next destination. I would highly recommend actually taking public transport or a taxi to the highest point that you can and then walking downhill to save yourself the physical labor. Alright, now it's time to chat about Edinburgh attractions. First off, make sure you're always booking ahead. These days you can book pretty much any of Edinburgh's major attractions online in advance, which really saves you a lot of time compared to trying to queue up for tickets. In peak season, a lot of the major attractions, like Edinburgh Castle, can also actually sell out for the entire month, so if there are any major attractions you really have your heart set on, I'd highly recommend booking those well in advance. Now in terms of how to get the best deals on attraction tickets in Edinburgh, make sure you know that not all sightseeing passes are created equal. For instance, the Edinburgh City Pass actually doesn't include a lot of the major sites, like Edinburgh Castle or Holyrood Palace. Instead, if you're hoping to visit the trio of Edinburgh's royal attractions like Edinburgh Castle, Holyrood Palace and the Royal Yacht Britannia, you should be looking into something called The Royal Edinburgh Ticket, which includes all three plus a bus tour for 65 pound. There are also some other passes and potential memberships that you can look into but honestly, those don't tend to be worth it unless you're going to be traveling around other places in Scotland besides Edinburgh. Now, while Edinburgh's main attractions can be quite pricey, one of my favorite things about the city is that there are so many cool attractions that are completely free. Some of my favorites include the Scottish National Museum, which is this stunning museum with an amazing terrace and also a huge collection of interesting items including Dolly the sheep. There's also the Scottish National Portrait Gallery, which is housed in the most stunning building and includes portraits and write ups on prominent historical figures from Scotland's history. And lastly, there's the Scottish Parliament, which is a very unique building that's close to Holyrood Palace and you can actually book a free tour to go see the inside, so that's definitely something that you should take advantage of if you're interested in architecture or politics because it's quite a unique place to go. There are also some smaller museums in the city that are run by Edinburgh City Council. I'll be honest, these museums are quite outdated and they're quite small and not as extravagant as some of the other attractions in the city but I do think they're worth visiting if you're interested in the particular subjects that the museums cover. So for instance, there's the Museum of Edinburgh which covers local history, there's the People's Story Museum, which covers the history of the city through the lens of the people that lived in it, there's also the Writer's Museum which is dedicated to some of Scotland's most famous writers, and there's the Museum of Childhood, which is the world's first museum dedicated to the concept of, you guessed it, childhood. So as you can see, it's actually quite simple to sightsee for free in Edinburgh, so don't get bogged down by all the paid attractions. Now a note on all things Harry Potter. So a lot of visitors come to Edinburgh thinking there's this huge connection between the city and Harry Potter in general but just to clear things up, Harry Potter never actually filmed in Edinburgh, so there's no filming locations for you to visit that you'll recognize from the movies. And while J. K. Rowling did live in Edinburgh while she was writing the books, she has herself famously debunked a lot of the popular myths surrounding Edinburgh and Harry Potter, for instance, the claim that Victoria Street is the street that inspired Diagon Alley or that the Elephant House Cafe is the birthplace of Harry Potter. So take what you hear about Harry Potter in Edinburgh with a grain of salt and just know that there is so much more to see in this city than just a vague connection to Harry Potter. Although I will say, those vaguely magic-themed shops are definitely taking over the city. Now another important must-know is that, despite the fact it's known as one of the top must-sees in Edinburgh, Arthur's Seat is actually quite a difficult hike, so don't pressure yourself to do it just because it's a must-do because during my time there I definitely saw many, many unprepared tourists who didn't realize just how steep the hike was going to be because it's still within the city but it is a massive extinct volcano, so it's not going to be an easy climb and so if you're not particularly athletic, don't pressure yourself into doing it because it is quite difficult. But if you do choose to do it, one thing I'll recommend is actually exiting and heading southeast instead of heading back into the city center, and the reason I recommend that is if you actually follow the path down southeast then you can end up at one of the oldest pubs in Scotland, if not the oldest, and it's called the Sheep Heid Pub and it's really cute, really cozy and does a fabulous roast dinner on Sundays. And on that note, don't be afraid to expand your sightseeing itinerary beyond the very city center because there are a lot of really cute neighborhoods and unique places to visit, kind of just outside of the main central core, so Stockbridge, for instance, is a very cute neighborhood with lots of lovely boutiques and cafes and bookshops, and Leith is also a very vibrant and dynamic part of the city with lots of cool food spots and pubs. Now on that note, let's move on to Edinburgh food and drink tips. First, you should know that Edinburgh has a very diverse food scene, with everything ranging from chippies selling deep-fried everything like pizza and Mars Bars, all the way to upscale seafood restaurants serving really refined Scottish fare, so definitely make the most of exploring the food scene while you're here. If you're interested in Scottish food there are definitely some fun must tries that I can highly recommend, I actually do really enjoy haggis, so I would recommend getting that, it's usually served with 'neeps and tatties which is mashed turnip and mashed potato, and there's also cullen skink which is a creamy fish soup. Now if you're looking for food recommendations what I can highly recommend is seeking out local Edinburgh foodies to get the scoop on what to eat because obviously, if they live in the city, they know best. So what I'll usually do, is I'll go on social media, either Instagram or TikTok, and look at people who live in Edinburgh, find foodie accounts, and kind of, get my food recommendations from there. Some really cool accounts that I can highly recommend include Fedinburgh, TopScottishScran and HannahScranz. Another must-know is to book ahead for restaurants. So oftentimes with popular spots, especially the ones that have been featured on social media, they can be booked out really far in advance, so make sure you book a table if there's a place that you really have your heart set on. Do take note though, that during festival season like in August, a lot of restaurants won't even take any bookings, just because that's how busy it gets. Now while in Edinburgh, I could also recommend booking some kind of whisky experience because after all, you are in Scotland, so why not give it a try? If you're not really a whisky person but you're curious to learn more, then what I would really highly recommend, rather than just doing a tasting, is actually booking a whisky experience where you learn more about the process that whisky is made and also how to properly enjoy it. In Edinburgh, there's two main popular ones that are quite touristy but also really well-known: the first one is the Scotch Whisky Experience, which is located right next to Edinburgh Castle and I've heard really great things about that, and more recently, there's the Johnny Walker Experience on Princes Street, which is one that I recently did and I hate to admit it because it was super cheesy but I loved it. As someone who didn't know a lot about whisky, I thought it was so much fun, it's very vibrant, very dynamic, a lot of multimedia, it's very slick and just overall, I totally left that experience like a Johnny Walker convert, like I genuinely bought a bunch of bottles at duty-free, so it is a bit like a brainwashing experience but it's also very fun and quite a fun way to get to know whisky in a very unintimidating way. If that's not what you're looking for though and you're looking for something quieter, maybe less over the top, then there's still a bunch of different tours and tastings that you can book, so the sky is really the limit when it comes to whisky tours in Edinburgh, be sure to put it on your list though, it's super fun. There is, of course, more to the local booze scene than just whisky though, in fact, gin is actually a pretty big deal in Edinburgh so if that's more your speed, you can actually tour distilleries for gin or get gin pretty much everywhere you go as well. Alright finally, let's move on to some Edinburgh tips for when you arrive. First of all, you should learn how to pronounce some commonly mispronounced names just in case you have to ask for directions or say any of the names out loud. For instance, this is pronounced Holly Rude, this is pronounced coburn, this neighborhood is leeth, and this is pronounced lowthian. There are also some common words you might come across that you might not know the meaning of, for instance, kirk means church, aye means yes, scran means food, tatty means potato and a dram is a small glass of a very alcoholic drink, so for instance, whisky. Next, you should know that jaywalking is very common in Edinburgh, there's a common joke actually that you can tell how local someone is to Edinburgh based on how impatient they are to cross the street. Most of the time, so long as there are no cars coming, locals will be crossing the street, so my word of advice is really to just stick with someone who looks like they know what they're doing and go with them. Another must-know is to dress for the weather by always bringing a rainproof and windproof jacket. Weather in Edinburgh is very unpredictable, so you might get sun one second and then rain and wind the next second. I think it's always good to be prepared because you never know when it's going to rain and it's also always better to bring a jacket versus an umbrella just because the Scottish wind is no joke. And bear in mind you will be walking a lot when you're in Edinburgh and those cobblestones can get slippery in the rain, so make sure you bring shoes that have a really good grip and don't be bringing shoes just because they're cute, and leave those heels at home. Lastly, when shopping for souvenirs I would say, stay away from those really touristy shops along the Royal Mile, just because a lot of the time, those stores are selling mass-produced goods that aren't necessarily of high quality, especially if you're buying things things like tartan scarves. Instead, what I would recommend is actually stopping in an independent shop instead and getting something that will be of higher quality and also probably be more unique than your standard souvenir shop stuff. One really cool place I came across was Tron Kirk, which is actually located on the Royal Mile as well and inside you'll find a Maker's Market where there's basically, different stall holders and they're all local makers who have Edinburgh-themed souvenirs and it's very cool and really worth taking a look. Alright, I hope you enjoyed those Edinburgh travel tips, thank you so much for watching and be sure to like and subscribe for more practical travel videos just like this one. I'll see you guys next week, bye!

videos.title: Edinburgh 1 Day Budget Tips | FREE & Cheap Things to DO, EAT & SEE | Scotland Travel Guide 2025
videos.description:
Planning a trip to Edinburgh on a budget? In this Scotland travel vlog 2025, I’ll show you exactly how to explore Edinburgh in 1 day with just £20! From the best cheap eats in Edinburgh to free attractions, secret spots, and must-see landmarks, this budget travel guide is packed with money-saving tips for travellers looking to experience the best of Scotland without breaking the bank.

Join me as I walk the historic Royal Mile, visit stunning sites like Edinburgh Castle, and uncover hidden gems locals love—all on a tight budget. Whether you're a solo traveller, a backpacker, or simply looking for inspiration for your next trip to Scotland, this vlog will help you plan the perfect one-day itinerary in Edinburgh.

💸 What to do in Edinburgh for free
🍔 Where to eat in Edinburgh on a budget
📍 Top attractions you can't miss
💡 Unusual things to do in Edinburgh
✈️ Scotland travel tips 2025

Don’t forget to like, comment, and subscribe for more UK travel guides, Scotland adventures, and budget-friendly itineraries.

*Donations are welcome in most places where entry is free :)
